summary:
- The "functional core, imperative shell" principle separates pure, testable business logic (core) from side-effect handling (shell), improving code quality and testability.
- Critics note the example (loading all data then filtering) is inefficient and risks shell penetration of the core; alternatives include database-side filtering and generators.
- Related concepts include "generic core, specific shell", "mechanism vs policy", and command-query separation, all emphasizing decoupling and separation of concerns.
- Benefits include faster development cycles and easier debugging, but challenges arise in defining boundaries and handling imperative operations like transactions.
